name: Dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker-induced peripheral edema treated with diuretics
clinical_concern: Edema is caused by precapillary arteriolar dilation without compensatory venous return enhancement; it is not volume-overload. Diuretics do not address the mechanism, may worsen neurohormonal activation, and increase electrolyte disturbance risk. Preferred intervention is CCB dose reduction or substitution with a renin-angiotensin system agent.
review_notes: Pharmacologically validated. DHP-CCB edema results from precapillary arteriolar dilation without postcapillary venodilation; it is NOT volume-overload. Diuretics do not reverse the mechanism, are ineffective long-term, and worsen neurohormonal activation. CCB dose reduction or substitution with a RAAS agent is the evidence-based first intervention. Well-described in ALLHAT and JNC guidelines. No field corrections required.

name: ACE inhibitor-induced cough treated with antitussives
clinical_concern: ACE inhibitor cough is caused by bradykinin and substance P accumulation due to inhibited angiotensin-converting enzyme degradation; antitussives are pharmacologically ineffective as definitive treatment and add adverse effects (sedation, constipation with codeine). The only definitive intervention is substitution with an ARB, which does not affect bradykinin metabolism.
review_notes: Well-established cascade. Bradykinin and substance P accumulation via ACE inhibition is the accepted mechanism. Antitussives are pharmacologically incapable of addressing the root cause. ARB substitution is the only definitive management. Incidence of ACE inhibitor cough: 5-20% (higher in Asian populations, up to 40%). No field corrections required.

name: Beta-blocker-induced depression treated with antidepressants
clinical_concern: Evidence for beta-blocker-induced depression is weak and contested in placebo-controlled RCTs; confounding by underlying cardiac disease is substantial. Evidence is marginally stronger for lipophilic agents that cross the blood-brain barrier (propranolol > metoprolol). Atenolol has been excluded from the trigger list: it is hydrophilic (LogP -0.23), has negligible CNS penetration, and lacks credible evidence for depression induction. Adding an antidepressant without reviewing beta-blocker lipophilicity and indication increases polypharmacy burden. Switch to a hydrophilic beta-blocker (atenolol, bisoprolol) should be considered before adding an antidepressant.
review_notes: Evidence for beta-blocker-induced depression is weak and contested. Placebo-controlled RCTs (Ko et al. 2002; Verbeek et al. 2012) have not demonstrated a significant causal link for the class as a whole; confounding by underlying cardiac disease is substantial. Evidence is marginally stronger for propranolol (highly lipophilic, highest CNS penetration) but remains low quality. CORRECTION APPLIED: 'atenolol' removed from trigger_drug — atenolol is hydrophilic (LogP -0.23), has negligible CNS penetration, and lacks credible evidence for depression induction. Retaining it would generate high false-positive alerts in elderly cardiac patients with comorbid depression. High false-positive risk: depression is common in all cardiovascular populations independent of beta-blocker use. Downgraded evidence_strength to 'low'.

name: Beta-blocker-induced erectile dysfunction treated with PDE5 inhibitors
clinical_concern: Beta-blockers reduce cardiac output and impair vasodilatory response in penile vasculature. PDE5 inhibitors cause additional vasodilation and hypotension, particularly dangerous in patients also on organic nitrates (absolute contraindication) or other antihypertensives. This cascade is frequent in cardiovascular patients and warrants structured medication review before PDE5 inhibitor initiation.
review_notes: Well-described cascade. Mechanism involves reduced cardiac output and impaired peripheral vasodilatory response in penile vasculature; psychological and psychogenic factors also contribute, making causality partially confounded. Evidence_strength set to 'moderate' (not high) because the causal link is partly confounded by underlying hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Critical safety flag: absolute contraindication of PDE5 inhibitors with organic nitrates is highly relevant in this cardiovascular population. No field corrections required.

name: Thiazide diuretic-induced hyperuricemia treated with urate-lowering agents
clinical_concern: Thiazides and their metabolites compete at URAT1 (SLC22A12, the primary urate reabsorber) and OAT4 (SLC22A11) in the proximal tubule, reducing net renal urate excretion. Adding urate-lowering therapy without reviewing the thiazide is a recognized cascade. Losartan has uricosuric properties via URAT1 inhibition and may be a more rational antihypertensive choice in patients with concurrent gout or hyperuricemia. Indomethacin for acute gout in this population adds renal and cardiovascular risk and should be avoided.
review_notes: Cascade is well-established and pharmacologically correct. MINOR MECHANISM CORRECTION applied to clinical_concern: the original text states 'OAT transporters' without specificity. The primary mechanism is competition at URAT1 (SLC22A12, the main urate reabsorber in the proximal tubule) by thiazide metabolites; OAT4 (SLC22A11) mediates the initial urate exchange step. Both are relevant. Updated to name URAT1/OAT4 explicitly for mechanistic precision. Losartan uricosuric properties (URAT1 inhibition) correctly noted. Indomethacin risk correctly flagged for gout management in this population.

name: Thiazide diuretic-induced hypokalemia treated with potassium supplements
clinical_concern: Adding potassium supplements or potassium-sparing diuretics without reviewing thiazide necessity is a recognized cascade. In patients with concurrent RAAS inhibition (ACE inhibitor, ARB, spironolactone), added potassium creates significant hyperkalemia risk. Concurrent hypomagnesemia (common with loop diuretics) prevents potassium repletion and must be corrected simultaneously.
review_notes: Well-established electrolyte cascade. Classified as 'monitoring-related' because potassium supplementation is often clinically appropriate and not inherently a cascade; the cascade occurs when thiazide-induced hypokalemia is not recognized as drug-induced and the thiazide is not reviewed. Spironolactone/eplerenone in HFrEF (RALES, EPHESUS evidence) may not represent an inappropriate cascade. 'Appropriateness' is context_dependent. Risk of hyperkalemia with concurrent RAAS inhibition correctly highlighted. Concurrent hypomagnesemia note (refractory hypokalemia) is clinically important and accurate. No field corrections required.

name: Thiazide diuretic-induced hyperglycemia treated with antidiabetic agents
clinical_concern: Thiazides impair insulin secretion via hypokalemia-mediated KATP channel hyperpolarization in pancreatic beta-cells and increase peripheral insulin resistance. Diabetogenic risk varies within the class: chlorthalidone carries the highest risk, hydrochlorothiazide intermediate, and indapamide (predominantly a vasodilator at low doses, 1.5 mg SR) substantially lower metabolic risk. Including indapamide as an equal trigger may inflate false-positive alerts. Adding antidiabetic therapy without reconsidering the thiazide and switching to a metabolically neutral antihypertensive (ACE inhibitor, ARB, DHP-CCB) or to indapamide adds metabolic complexity and pill burden unnecessarily.
review_notes: Cascade is pharmacologically valid. CORRECTION: indapamide has substantially lower diabetogenic risk than hydrochlorothiazide or chlorthalidone. Indapamide acts primarily as a vasodilator at low doses (1.5 mg SR) with modest diuretic effect and minimal metabolic impact; its inclusion as an equal trigger alongside chlorthalidone (highest diabetogenic risk) inflates false-positive alerting. Clinical_concern updated to stratify within the thiazide class. Hypokalemia-mediated beta-cell hyperpolarization mechanism is accurate (KATP channel hyperpolarization impairs Ca2+-triggered insulin exocytosis).

name: Amiodarone-induced hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine
clinical_concern: Amiodarone contains approximately 37% iodine by weight. Amiodarone-induced hypothyroidism (AIH) results primarily from two distinct mechanisms: (1) the Wolff-Chaikoff effect — excess iodine load inhibits thyroid organification in iodine-replete individuals, impairing T3/T4 synthesis; (2) direct cytotoxic or immune-mediated thyroid damage from amiodarone and its active metabolite desethylamiodarone. Note: type 1 deiodinase inhibition by amiodarone causes T4→T3 conversion impairment and produces elevated T4 with low T3 (low T3 syndrome), which is a separate phenomenon from overt hypothyroidism and should not be conflated with AIH. Amiodarone has a half-life of approximately 40-100 days; thyroid dysfunction may persist for months after discontinuation. Adding levothyroxine without reassessing amiodarone indication is a recognized cascade, particularly in elderly patients.
review_notes: MECHANISTIC CORRECTION REQUIRED. The original clinical_concern conflates two distinct mechanisms: (1) Type 1 deiodinase inhibition by amiodarone/desethylamiodarone causes impaired T4→T3 conversion, producing elevated T4 and low T3 — this does NOT directly cause hypothyroidism; it causes 'low T3 syndrome'. (2) Amiodarone-induced hypothyroidism (AIH) is primarily caused by the Wolff-Chaikoff effect (excess iodine load inhibits thyroid organification — particularly in iodine-replete populations) and/or direct cytotoxic/immune-mediated thyroid damage. These are separate and not interchangeable. The original text incorrectly attributes AIH to 'type 1 deiodinase inhibition (Wolff-Chaikoff effect)' — combining two distinct mechanisms under one label. Clinical_concern corrected to separate these mechanisms. Classified as 'monitoring-related' because levothyroxine is often appropriate when amiodarone cannot be discontinued.

name: Alpha-1 adrenergic blocker-induced orthostatic hypotension treated with vasopressors
clinical_concern: Non-selective alpha-1 blockers (doxazosin, terazosin, prazosin) reduce sympathetic vascular tone via alpha-1A and alpha-1B blockade, causing venous pooling and orthostatic hypotension — high risk. Uro-selective alpha-1A antagonists (tamsulosin, silodosin) have substantially lower risk of systemic orthostatic hypotension due to high urothelial selectivity; alert weight should be differentiated in CDS implementation. Adding midodrine or fludrocortisone without reviewing alpha-blocker indication (BPH vs. hypertension), agent selection, and dose substantially increases cardiovascular burden and supine hypertension risk in elderly patients. Reviewing the indication, switching to a uro-selective agent, or dose reduction should precede vasopressor addition.
review_notes: Cascade is pharmacologically valid for non-selective alpha-1 blockers. CORRECTION: Uro-selective alpha-1A antagonists (tamsulosin, silodosin) have substantially lower risk of systemic orthostatic hypotension compared to non-selective agents (doxazosin, terazosin, prazosin) due to high affinity for alpha-1A subtype (predominant in prostate) vs alpha-1B (predominant in vasculature). Including all agents at equal alert weight would generate high false-positive rates, particularly for tamsulosin which is among the most prescribed drugs for BPH. Clinical_concern updated to stratify by receptor selectivity. False-positive risk: orthostatic hypotension is common in elderly patients independent of alpha-blockers.

name: Statin-induced myalgia treated with analgesics or NSAIDs
clinical_concern: Statin-induced muscle symptoms affect 5-10% of patients, mediated by CoQ10 depletion and mitochondrial dysfunction. NSAIDs add GI, cardiovascular and renal risk without addressing the cause. CK should be measured; significant elevation warrants statin discontinuation. Switching to a hydrophilic statin (rosuvastatin, pravastatin) or alternate-day dosing often resolves symptoms without requiring analgesics.
review_notes: Cascade is valid and clinically relevant. Classified 'context-dependent' because not all muscle pain in a statin user is statin-induced; baseline CK, symptom timeline, and exclusion of other causes are required before attributing myalgia to the statin. Mechanism note: CoQ10 depletion is a proposed but not definitively proven mechanism; impaired geranylgeranyl pyrophosphate synthesis (from mevalonate pathway blockade) with secondary mitochondrial dysfunction is better supported. Evidence_strength 'moderate' reflects the frequency of nocebo effect and difficulty establishing causality. No field corrections required.

name: SSRI/SNRI-induced SIADH treated with hyponatremia correction agents
clinical_concern: SSRI/SNRI-induced SIADH is underrecognized and prevalent in elderly populations. Incidence of any hyponatremia (Na <135 mEq/L) in unselected elderly outpatients is approximately 10-15%; clinically significant hyponatremia (Na <130 mEq/L) occurs in approximately 1-4% of SSRI users overall, rising to 20-30% only in highly selected high-risk groups (elderly women, concurrent thiazide, low baseline sodium, low BMI). Initiating vasopressin receptor antagonists or sodium supplementation without identifying and withdrawing the causative SSRI/SNRI delays appropriate management. Sodium must be corrected at a safe rate (<10 mEq/L per 24h) to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome.
review_notes: Cascade is pharmacologically valid and well-established. CORRECTION: The stated incidence 'up to 30% in high-risk populations' requires contextualization. General elderly population incidence of clinically significant hyponatremia (Na <130 mEq/L) with SSRIs is approximately 1–4%; incidence of any hyponatremia (Na <135 mEq/L) reaches 10–15% in unselected elderly outpatients and up to 20-30% only in highly selected high-risk groups (elderly women, concurrent thiazide, low baseline sodium, low BMI). The unqualified '30%' figure risks alert fatigue. Clinical_concern updated to specify risk stratification. Sodium correction rate limit (<10 mEq/L/24h) to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome is critical and correctly stated.

name: Antipsychotic-induced extrapyramidal symptoms treated with anticholinergic agents
clinical_concern: Adding anticholinergics to antipsychotics dramatically increases total anticholinergic burden, causing cognitive impairment, urinary retention, constipation, and delirium — especially in elderly patients. Validated scales (ACB, ARS) should quantify total burden. Preferred interventions include antipsychotic dose reduction, switch to lower-EPS-risk agents (quetiapine, clozapine), or use of amantadine which has lower anticholinergic burden.
review_notes: Well-established cascade with strong geriatric pharmacotherapy evidence. Note: aripiprazole (partial D2 agonist) causes EPS — predominantly akathisia — less commonly than full D2 antagonists, and drug-induced parkinsonism/dystonia is rare at standard doses with aripiprazole. Its inclusion is acceptable but alert weight should be lower than for haloperidol or fluphenazine. Anticholinergic burden quantification (ACB scale, ARS) correctly emphasized. Amantadine (NMDA antagonist, lower ACB) as an alternative is pharmacologically correct. No field corrections required.

name: Antipsychotic-induced hyperprolactinemia treated with dopamine agonists
clinical_concern: This cascade creates a direct pharmacodynamic conflict: dopamine agonists may antagonize antipsychotic D2 blockade, potentially worsening psychosis. Adding cabergoline or bromocriptine without reviewing antipsychotic choice is clinically inappropriate. Switching to prolactin-sparing antipsychotics (quetiapine, aripiprazole, clozapine, olanzapine) is the recommended primary intervention.
review_notes: Critical pharmacodynamic conflict cascade. Dopamine agonists (cabergoline, bromocriptine) directly antagonize antipsychotic D2 blockade at the nigrostriatal and mesolimbic pathways — risk of psychotic relapse is real and documented. This cascade should trigger a high-priority alert requiring prescriber review. Prolactin-sparing switch (quetiapine, aripiprazole, clozapine, olanzapine) is the mechanistically correct primary intervention. No field corrections required.

name: Antipsychotic/dopamine antagonist-induced akathisia treated with beta-blockers or benzodiazepines
clinical_concern: Akathisia is frequently misidentified as anxiety or psychotic agitation, prompting antipsychotic dose escalation which worsens the akathisia — a particularly dangerous feedback loop. Benzodiazepines increase sedation, respiratory depression, and fall risk. Propranolol is effective but contraindicated in asthma and heart block. Dose reduction of the causative agent should precede any pharmacological treatment of akathisia.
review_notes: Classified 'context-dependent' because propranolol for antipsychotic-induced akathisia is a recognized evidence-based second-line treatment when dose reduction is not clinically feasible (RCT evidence exists). The cascade concern is valid specifically when: (a) akathisia is misidentified as anxiety/agitation leading to antipsychotic dose escalation (worsening akathisia — a critical feedback loop), or (b) benzodiazepines are added as a reflex without reviewing the antipsychotic. Metoclopramide in the trigger_drug list is important (common underrecognized cause of akathisia in non-psychiatric settings). No field corrections required.

name: Lithium-induced hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine
clinical_concern: Lithium inhibits thyroid hormone synthesis and release by blocking iodine transport and thyroglobulin proteolysis. Hypothyroidism occurs in up to 40% of long-term users, more commonly in women. While levothyroxine may be appropriate when lithium cannot be discontinued, this cascade occurs when thyroid function is not monitored regularly (recommended every 6 months) and the drug-induced nature of hypothyroidism is unrecognized.
review_notes: Well-established cascade. Lithium inhibits thyroidal sodium-iodide symporter, thyroglobulin proteolysis, and thyroid hormone release. Adding levothyroxine is often appropriate when lithium cannot be discontinued for mood stability; the cascade occurs when the drug-induced etiology is unrecognized and lithium is not reconsidered. Biannual TSH monitoring is standard of care and prevents late presentation. Incidence of 'up to 40%' is within published range (20-42% in long-term female users). Classified 'monitoring-related': appropriate monitoring prevents cascade. No field corrections required.

name: Lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus treated with amiloride or other agents
clinical_concern: Lithium accumulates in collecting duct principal cells via ENaC channels, impairing aquaporin-2 expression and water reabsorption. Amiloride blocks ENaC, reducing lithium entry — mechanistically rational but adds a drug. Indomethacin significantly reduces renal lithium clearance, increasing lithium toxicity risk. This cascade requires meticulous monitoring of lithium levels, renal function, and electrolytes.
review_notes: Pharmacologically valid. Amiloride mechanism (ENaC blockade reducing lithium entry into collecting duct principal cells) is mechanistically rational and evidence-supported (Batlle et al.). Indomethacin-lithium interaction (reduced renal clearance via PGE2 inhibition → lithium toxicity) is a critical safety flag and correctly noted. HCTZ paradoxical effect (volume contraction → compensatory proximal Na/Li reabsorption reducing distal delivery) is mechanistically accurate. Evidence_strength 'moderate': clinical evidence is based on small trials and case series. No field corrections required.

name: Valproate-induced postural tremor treated with beta-blockers
clinical_concern: Valproate-induced tremor is dose-dependent and resolves with dose reduction in most cases. Adding propranolol introduces cardiovascular and pulmonary risks; primidone may cause significant pharmacokinetic interactions with valproate and add sedation. The appropriate primary intervention is valproate dose reduction or switch to extended-release formulation, not addition of a new drug.
review_notes: Cascade is valid and clinically relevant. Valproate tremor is dose-dependent and typically resolves with dose reduction (to <40 mg/kg/day) or extended-release formulation in 50-70% of cases. Adding propranolol without dose review is inappropriate. Note on primidone-valproate interaction: phenobarbital (primidone metabolite) is a CYP inducer but effects on valproate (primarily glucuronidated) are modest; the more relevant concern is additive CNS sedation. Evidence_strength 'moderate' as the cascade is well-described but specific cascade studies are limited. No field corrections required.

name: SSRI/SNRI-induced bruxism treated with benzodiazepines or buspirone
clinical_concern: Serotonin-mediated inhibition of dopaminergic pathways disrupts motor control during sleep, causing bruxism. Clonazepam increases fall risk, sedation, and carries dependency risk particularly in elderly patients. Buspirone (5-HT1A partial agonist) has some evidence for reversing SSRI-induced bruxism and a more favorable risk profile. Dose reduction of the causative SSRI/SNRI should be considered as the primary intervention.
review_notes: Evidence base is weaker than presented. SSRI-induced bruxism is documented predominantly in case reports and small observational series; systematic evidence is limited. The proposed mechanism (serotonergic inhibition of dopaminergic motor control pathways) is pharmacologically plausible but not confirmed. Buspirone for treatment has evidence only from isolated case reports and one small series — it cannot be reliably recommended without additional data. Evidence_strength downgraded to 'low'. Clonazepam benzodiazepine risks (dependence, falls, cognitive impairment) are accurately and importantly noted. Gabapentin inclusion as treatment is not well-supported by specific bruxism data. High false-positive risk: bruxism is multifactorial (anxiety, dental malocclusion, caffeine, idiopathic) and temporal relationship with SSRI must be carefully established.

name: Cholinesterase inhibitor-induced nausea and vomiting treated with antiemetics
clinical_concern: Cholinesterase inhibitor GI effects are dose-dependent and cholinergically mediated. CRITICAL CDS STRATIFICATION: (1) metoclopramide and prochlorperazine are centrally-acting D2 antagonists — they directly oppose the mechanism of AChEIs, worsen cognitive function in dementia patients, and increase extrapyramidal symptoms; these represent a CONTRAINDICATED cascade combination. (2) Domperidone (peripheral D2 antagonist) and ondansetron (5-HT3 antagonist) are relatively safer antiemetic options and should trigger a REVIEW-level alert rather than a contraindication. Preferred management for AChEI-induced nausea: slow titration, dose reduction, or switch to transdermal rivastigmine.
review_notes: Cascade is pharmacologically valid. CRITICAL CDS IMPLEMENTATION NOTE: The treatment_drug list mixes contraindicated antiemetics (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine) with relatively acceptable alternatives (domperidone, ondansetron). This distinction is clinically essential and must be reflected in alert stratification: (1) metoclopramide and prochlorperazine in dementia patients are absolutely contraindicated — central D2 antagonism worsens cognitive function and directly opposes AChEI mechanism; alert level: CRITICAL/CONTRAINDICATED. (2) Domperidone and ondansetron are safer alternatives with peripheral mechanisms — alert level: WARNING/REVIEW. Clinical_concern updated and treatment_drug field restructured to separate these subgroups. This cascade could cause significant clinical harm if all treatment_drugs trigger the same alert level.

name: Levodopa-induced nausea treated with centrally-acting dopamine antagonist antiemetics
clinical_concern: Centrally-acting dopamine antagonist antiemetics (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, chlorpromazine, promethazine, haloperidol) are absolutely contraindicated in Parkinson disease. These agents block nigrostriatal D2 receptors and cause severe motor deterioration, potentially irreversible worsening of parkinsonism, and acute dystonic reactions. This cascade is particularly dangerous because Parkinson disease may be undiagnosed, undertreated, or unknown to the prescribing clinician (e.g. prescriber outside neurology). SAFE ALTERNATIVES for nausea in Parkinson patients: domperidone (peripheral D2 antagonist, limited CNS penetration; monitor QTc) or ondansetron (5-HT3 antagonist, no D2 activity). A structured medication history is mandatory before prescribing any antiemetic to a patient with Parkinson disease or on levodopa.
review_notes: CRITICAL STRUCTURAL ERROR CORRECTED. The original treatment_drug list ['domperidone', 'ondansetron'] contained the SAFE/APPROPRIATE antiemetics for Parkinson patients — these represent correct clinical management, NOT the prescribing cascade. The cascade drugs are the CENTRALLY-ACTING dopamine antagonist antiemetics (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, chlorpromazine, promethazine, haloperidol) which block nigrostriatal D2 receptors and cause severe motor deterioration in PD patients. Name, treatment_drug, treatment_class, and clinical_concern corrected accordingly. This was the most significant structural error in the dataset. If deployed uncorrected, the CDS tool would alert on appropriate prescribing (domperidone use in PD) while missing the truly dangerous cascade (metoclopramide use in PD). Domperidone and ondansetron are noted in clinical_concern as the recommended safe alternatives.

name: NSAID-induced hypertension treated with antihypertensives
clinical_concern: NSAIDs inhibit renal prostaglandin synthesis (PGE2, PGI2), causing sodium and water retention and directly blunting the effect of diuretics, ACE inhibitors, and ARBs. Adding antihypertensives without removing the NSAID is highly prevalent and futile as long as the NSAID continues. COX-2 selective inhibitors carry equivalent or greater cardiovascular risk. Paracetamol or topical NSAIDs are safer alternatives for pain.
review_notes: Well-established cascade with substantial pharmacoepidemiological evidence. Mechanism (PGE2/PGI2 inhibition → sodium/water retention → blunting of RAAS-inhibitor and diuretic effects) is pharmacologically accurate. COX-2 selective inhibitors carry equivalent or greater cardiovascular risk (VIGOR, APPROVe trials) — correctly noted. Paracetamol at standard doses has modest blood pressure effects and is generally preferred for mild-moderate pain in cardiovascular patients. No field corrections required.

name: NSAID-induced peripheral edema treated with diuretics
clinical_concern: NSAIDs promote sodium and water retention via renal prostaglandin inhibition. Diuretics are largely ineffective while the NSAID is continued. The triple whammy combination (NSAID + diuretic + ACE inhibitor or ARB) is a well-documented cause of acute kidney injury in outpatient and hospital settings, particularly in elderly or volume-depleted patients. NSAID withdrawal should be the primary intervention.
review_notes: Well-established cascade. Triple whammy nephrotoxicity (NSAID + diuretic + ACE inhibitor/ARB) is a well-documented cause of acute kidney injury in ambulatory and hospitalized elderly patients. The mechanism (PGE2 inhibition → sodium retention → diuretic inefficacy) is pharmacologically sound. NSAID withdrawal should be the primary intervention. Closely related to CC061; both should alert simultaneously in polypharmacy patients. No field corrections required.

name: Systemic corticosteroid-induced hyperglycemia treated with antidiabetic agents
clinical_concern: Corticosteroids elevate postprandial glucose predominantly via hepatic gluconeogenesis stimulation and peripheral insulin resistance; fasting glucose may be minimally affected. CONTEXT IS ESSENTIAL: in patients on long-term or high-dose steroids (>7.5 mg prednisolone equivalent/day), antidiabetic treatment is clinically appropriate and necessary — this is not a cascade. The cascade occurs when: (a) short-course steroids cause transient hyperglycemia leading to initiation of antidiabetics that persist after steroid discontinuation, or (b) the postprandial-predominant pattern is not recognized and fasting-targeted basal insulin is the only intervention. NPH insulin timed with the steroid dose, or premixed insulin, is more mechanistically appropriate. Antidiabetic treatment must be reviewed with each steroid dose change or taper.
review_notes: Cascade is pharmacologically valid but appropriateness is context-dependent. CLASSIFICATION CORRECTION: In patients on long-term or high-dose steroids, antidiabetic treatment is clinically necessary and appropriate (not a cascade). The cascade occurs specifically when: (a) short-course steroids cause transient hyperglycemia leading to initiation of antidiabetics that persist after steroid discontinuation, or (b) the postprandial-predominant pattern of steroid diabetes is not recognized and inappropriate regimens are used. Mechanism is correctly described: postprandial hyperglycemia via hepatic gluconeogenesis stimulation and peripheral insulin resistance. NPH insulin co-timed with steroid dose or premixed insulin is more mechanistically appropriate than basal-only regimens — correctly noted.

name: Systemic corticosteroid-induced hypertension treated with antihypertensives
clinical_concern: Corticosteroids exert mineralocorticoid-like effects causing sodium retention and volume expansion; mineralocorticoid potency varies across agents (fludrocortisone > prednisolone > dexamethasone). Spironolactone may partially counteract mineralocorticoid effects but carries hyperkalemia risk with RAAS inhibitors. Steroid dose minimization and the possibility of steroid-sparing disease-modifying therapy should be part of the therapeutic strategy.
review_notes: Cascade is valid and pharmacologically correct. Classified 'context-dependent' because antihypertensive treatment is often appropriate in long-term steroid users; cascade occurs when drug-induced etiology is not recognized and steroid tapering/sparing is not pursued. Mineralocorticoid potency order is correct (fludrocortisone >> prednisolone > dexamethasone). Minor omission: hydrocortisone has significant mineralocorticoid activity (comparable to prednisolone) but is not included in the comparison — acceptable as context note. Evidence_strength 'moderate': well-described mechanism but cascade-specific epidemiological data are limited. No field corrections required.

name: Oral bisphosphonate-induced upper GI irritation treated with proton pump inhibitors
clinical_concern: Oral bisphosphonates cause upper GI irritation and esophagitis via direct chemical contact: the tablet or residue adheres to the esophageal mucosa, causing localized chemical irritation, disruption of mucosal cell membrane integrity (amphiphilic properties at low pH), and mucosal inflammation. This is a contact-mediated effect independent of systemic absorption. Adding a PPI instead of reviewing administration technique (strict fasting, upright posture, 200 mL water, 30-60 minute pre-meal delay) or switching to IV zoledronic acid adds drug burden. Critically: long-term PPI use independently reduces bone mineral density (impaired calcium and magnesium absorption) and causes hypomagnesemia, compounding the indication for which bisphosphonate was prescribed.
review_notes: MECHANISM CORRECTION: The original clinical_concern cites 'inhibition of osteoclast-like activity in GI epithelium' as a mechanism of bisphosphonate GI toxicity. This is not an established pharmacological mechanism in the current literature. The accepted primary mechanism is direct chemical contact between the bisphosphonate tablet/residue and esophageal/gastric mucosa, causing localized chemical irritation, disruption of mucosal cell membranes (due to their amphiphilic properties at low pH), and mucosal inflammation. This does not involve osteoclast-like cells in the GI tract. The unsupported mechanism has been removed from clinical_concern. PPI-bisphosphonate cascade concern (bone density worsening, hypomagnesemia from PPIs) is pharmacologically valid and important.

name: Second-generation antipsychotic-induced metabolic syndrome treated with antidiabetic or weight-loss agents
ade: metabolic syndrome / weight gain / insulin resistance
clinical_concern: SGA-induced metabolic syndrome involves H1 histaminergic and M3 muscarinic receptor antagonism combined with serotonergic and dopaminergic modulation of hypothalamic satiety pathways. Adding metabolic agents without reviewing antipsychotic choice perpetuates the root cause. Switch to metabolically neutral agents (aripiprazole, ziprasidone, lurasidone) should be formally evaluated before initiating additional pharmacotherapy. Note: GLP-1 receptor agonists (liraglutide, semaglutide) have emerging evidence for SGA-induced weight management but add injection burden and GI side effects.
review_notes: Cascade is pharmacologically valid and well-documented. CORRECTION 1: 'aripiprazole adjunct' removed from treatment_drug. Aripiprazole is prescribed to treat the underlying psychiatric indication (as a switch strategy to a metabolically neutral antipsychotic), not to treat metabolic syndrome as a pharmacological target. Its inclusion in treatment_drug is conceptually incorrect for a CDS cascade detection system. CORRECTION 2: 'glucagon-like peptide-1 agonist' replaced with specific INN drug names (liraglutide, semaglutide) for machine readability. Mechanism (H1 histaminergic and M3 muscarinic antagonism + hypothalamic satiety pathway disruption) is pharmacologically correct. Ziprasidone, lurasidone, aripiprazole as metabolically neutral alternatives are correctly identified.

name: Gabapentinoid-induced peripheral edema treated with diuretics
clinical_concern: Gabapentinoid edema is caused by increased microvascular permeability rather than cardiac or volume-overload mechanisms; diuretics are largely ineffective and add electrolyte disturbance risk. Edema often responds to dose reduction or drug discontinuation. This cascade is increasingly relevant given the substantial rise in gabapentinoid prescribing for neuropathic pain, anxiety, and fibromyalgia.
review_notes: Cascade is pharmacologically valid and increasingly relevant given widespread gabapentinoid prescribing. Mechanism (increased microvascular permeability via alpha-2-delta subunit modulation in peripheral vasculature) is plausible; the edema is not hemodynamic/cardiac in origin, making diuretics ineffective. Evidence_strength 'moderate' because the precise mechanism of gabapentinoid edema is not fully elucidated and clinical studies are observational. Dose reduction typically resolves edema in most patients. No field corrections required.

name: Proton pump inhibitor-induced hypomagnesemia treated with magnesium supplements
clinical_concern: Chronic PPI use (>1 year) impairs active transcellular magnesium absorption in the small intestine via TRPM6/TRPM7 channel downregulation. Oral supplements have limited bioavailability when the PPI is continued; IV replacement may be required acutely. Recurrent hypomagnesemia is a strong indicator for PPI deprescription review. Concurrent loop diuretic use compounds magnesium losses.
review_notes: Well-established cascade, particularly relevant with long-term PPI use (>1 year). TRPM6/TRPM7 channel downregulation mechanism is supported by in vitro and clinical studies. Magnesium supplementation is context-dependent: it is appropriate as acute treatment but oral bioavailability is substantially limited while the PPI continues (IV required for acute severe hypomagnesemia). The cascade occurs when PPI deprescription is not evaluated as a primary intervention. Synergistic magnesium depletion with loop diuretics is accurately and importantly noted. No field corrections required.

name: Dopamine antagonist antiemetic-induced parkinsonism misdiagnosed as Parkinson disease, treated with levodopa
clinical_concern: Drug-induced parkinsonism (DIP) caused by central D2 antagonists (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, chlorpromazine, haloperidol) is clinically indistinguishable from idiopathic Parkinson disease and is frequently misdiagnosed, leading to initiation of levodopa or dopamine agonists. Initiating antiparkinsonian therapy without identifying and withdrawing the causative agent is serious; symptoms may require weeks to months to resolve after drug withdrawal. Note: domperidone is NOT included as a trigger — it is a peripheral D2 antagonist with limited CNS penetration at standard doses and substantially lower DIP risk than central D2 blockers; it should not co-trigger this cascade alert. DaT-SPECT can help differentiate DIP from idiopathic PD, but a complete medication history is the essential first step.
review_notes: CRITICAL PHARMACOLOGY FIX APPLIED (final version): domperidone removed from trigger_drug per CDS integration requirement. Domperidone is a peripheral D2 antagonist with negligible CNS penetration at standard doses; its risk for causing drug-induced parkinsonism is substantially lower than central D2 antagonists (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, chlorpromazine, haloperidol). Including domperidone as an equal trigger alongside metoclopramide would generate unacceptable false-positive alerts and — critically — wrongly flag domperidone as unsafe in Parkinson patients where it is the PREFERRED antiemetic. Clinical_concern updated to note its absence from the trigger list and clarify its status as a safe alternative in this context.

name: Anticholinergic drug-induced constipation treated with laxatives
clinical_concern: Anticholinergics reduce colonic motility via M3 muscarinic receptor blockade throughout the GI tract. Adding laxatives without assessing total anticholinergic burden (ACB score, ARS) is a high-frequency cascade in elderly and polypharmacy patients. In severe cases, anticholinergic-induced ileus may mask acute abdominal pathology. Total anticholinergic load should be quantified and minimized.
review_notes: Well-established cascade with strong geriatric pharmacotherapy evidence. M3 muscarinic receptor blockade throughout the GI tract is the correct mechanism. ACB (Anticholinergic Cognitive Burden) and ARS (Anticholinergic Risk Scale) are validated tools for quantifying total burden — correctly referenced. Risk of anticholinergic ileus masking acute surgical abdomen is accurately noted. This cascade often nests within CC052 or CC084 (other anticholinergic cascades) in polypharmacy patients. No field corrections required.

name: Mirabegron-induced hypertension treated with antihypertensives
clinical_concern: Mirabegron activates beta-3 adrenergic receptors on bladder detrusor muscle, causing relaxation and increasing bladder capacity. A clinically observed but modest increase in blood pressure (mean SBP +1-2 mmHg per FDA label) has been documented, particularly at higher doses. Mechanism note: vascular beta-3 receptor activation stimulates eNOS and causes vasodilation — not vasoconstriction. The hypertensive effect may involve cardiac beta-3 stimulation (increased inotropy/chronotropy) or other sympathomimetic mechanisms. Mirabegron is contraindicated in severe uncontrolled hypertension (SBP >180 mmHg or DBP >110 mmHg). Blood pressure monitoring at initiation is required; mirabegron discontinuation and alternative OAB treatment review is preferred over adding an antihypertensive. Vibegron has greater bladder selectivity.
review_notes: MECHANISTIC CORRECTION REQUIRED. The original clinical_concern states 'off-target effects on vascular beta-3 receptors causing vasoconstriction and hypertension.' This is pharmacologically incorrect. Beta-3 adrenergic receptor activation in vascular endothelium stimulates eNOS activity, producing nitric oxide and causing VASODILATION — not vasoconstriction. The clinical observation that mirabegron can raise blood pressure (FDA label: mean SBP increase ~1-2 mmHg) is real and documented, but the mechanism is not vascular beta-3 mediated vasoconstriction. Possible mechanisms include cardiac beta-3 stimulation (modest positive inotropy/chronotropy) or indirect sympathomimetic effects. Clinical_concern corrected. Vibegron (more bladder-selective, less systemic beta-3 stimulation) has a potentially better cardiovascular safety profile — relevant for CDS context. Evidence_strength 'moderate'.

name: HIV protease inhibitor-induced dyslipidemia treated with statins
clinical_concern: Ritonavir-boosted protease inhibitors cause dyslipidemia via impaired lipid clearance and adipokine dysregulation. Critical DDI: ritonavir is a potent CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein inhibitor. Simvastatin and lovastatin are contraindicated (severe rhabdomyolysis risk). Preferred statins are pravastatin (not CYP3A4-metabolized) or pitavastatin. Rosuvastatin levels are increased by some PIs via OATP1B1. ART regimen review for switch to integrase-based, non-boosted regimens should precede statin initiation.
review_notes: Well-established cascade with critical DDI dimension. Classified 'context_dependent' for appropriateness: statins may be genuinely indicated (cardiovascular risk management is important in PLHIV); the cascade concern is DDI risk and failure to optimize ART before adding a statin. CYP3A4 interactions are correctly described: simvastatin and lovastatin are absolute contraindications with ritonavir-boosted regimens. Rosuvastatin-OATP1B1 interaction is accurate for lopinavir/ritonavir (2-5x increase in rosuvastatin AUC). Pitavastatin (minimal CYP metabolism) is the preferred statin in PLHIV. No field corrections required.

name: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate-induced renal tubular dysfunction treated with mineral supplements
clinical_concern: TDF causes mitochondrial toxicity in renal proximal tubular cells, potentially leading to full Fanconi syndrome (phosphaturia, glycosuria, proteinuria, aminoaciduria). Supplementing phosphate and vitamin D without switching to tenofovir alafenamide (TAF) — which has substantially lower renal and bone toxicity due to intracellular prodrug activation — fails to address the mechanism and allows progressive renal and bone damage. Routine monitoring of eGFR, phosphate, urine protein/creatinine ratio, and bone density is essential.
review_notes: Well-established cascade relevant to aging PLHIV. TDF mitochondrial toxicity mechanism in proximal tubular cells is pharmacologically correct. TAF (tenofovir alafenamide) comparison is clinically accurate: TAF achieves 90% lower plasma tenofovir concentrations due to intracellular prodrug activation, substantially reducing renal and bone toxicity. Fanconi syndrome manifestations are correctly listed. Monitoring protocol (eGFR, phosphate, urine protein/creatinine ratio, bone density) reflects current guidelines. No field corrections required.

name: Efavirenz-induced neuropsychiatric symptoms treated with antidepressants
clinical_concern: Efavirenz causes CNS adverse effects in up to 50% of patients partly via CYP2B6 slow-metabolizer pharmacogenetics, leading to elevated drug exposure. Adding an antidepressant without switching ART ignores the pharmacological cause and adds DDI risk (CYP2B6 interactions). Modern integrase strand transfer inhibitor-based regimens (dolutegravir, bictegravir) have substantially better CNS tolerability and should be preferred. CYP2B6 genotyping informs risk stratification.
review_notes: Well-established cascade, especially relevant in aging PLHIV. CYP2B6 pharmacogenetics (slow metabolizer phenotype: CYP2B6 516G>T) as a predictor of efavirenz CNS toxicity is well-documented. DDI context: bupropion is also a CYP2B6 substrate — caution warranted in slow metabolizers. Venlafaxine inhibits CYP2D6 (not CYP2B6); efavirenz induces CYP2D6, potentially reducing venlafaxine levels. These specific DDIs add nuance to antidepressant selection. Switch to integrase-based regimens (dolutegravir, bictegravir) as primary intervention is evidence-based and correctly emphasized. No field corrections required.

name: Protease inhibitor-based antiretroviral therapy-induced insulin resistance treated with antidiabetic agents
clinical_concern: Older HIV protease inhibitors (especially indinavir) directly inhibit GLUT4 transporter function, acutely impairing insulin-stimulated peripheral glucose uptake. This is compounded by lipodystrophy and visceral fat accumulation. Newer integrase-based regimens have substantially lower metabolic effects. ART regimen optimization — switching away from boosted older PIs — should be formally evaluated before initiating antidiabetic pharmacotherapy.
review_notes: Cascade is pharmacologically valid. Mechanism note: GLUT4 inhibition is established primarily for indinavir (direct inhibitor) and nelfinavir. For lopinavir/ritonavir, the predominant metabolic mechanism involves adipocyte dysfunction, increased lipolysis, and impaired adipokine (adiponectin, leptin) signaling rather than direct GLUT4 inhibition — a nuance not captured in the original but not an error given the general framing. Modern integrase-based regimens (dolutegravir, bictegravir, raltegravir) have substantially lower metabolic effects — correctly emphasized as the primary intervention. No field corrections required.

name: Calcineurin inhibitor-induced hypertension treated with calcium channel blockers
clinical_concern: Non-dihydropyridine CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil) significantly increase calcineurin inhibitor exposure via strong CYP3A4 inhibition, markedly raising nephrotoxicity and immunosuppression toxicity risk. Dihydropyridine CCBs (amlodipine) are preferred but modestly increase cyclosporine levels via CYP3A4 — therapeutic drug monitoring adjustment is required. This is a bidirectionally relevant pharmacokinetic cascade.
review_notes: Well-established cascade with bidirectional pharmacokinetic relevance. Non-DHP CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil) as CYP3A4 inhibitors raise calcineurin inhibitor levels significantly: diltiazem increases cyclosporine AUC by 30-60%; verapamil by 40-100%. Therapeutic drug monitoring is mandatory with any CCB addition. Classified 'context_dependent' because amlodipine is often the preferred antihypertensive in transplant patients and is clinically appropriate with appropriate TDM; the cascade concern is DDI risk with non-DHP CCBs and failure to monitor. No field corrections required.

name: Tacrolimus-induced post-transplant diabetes mellitus treated with antidiabetic agents
clinical_concern: Tacrolimus impairs pancreatic beta-cell insulin secretion via calcineurin-dependent inhibition of NFAT transcription factors regulating insulin gene expression. PTDM is multifactorial (tacrolimus + concurrent corticosteroids). Tacrolimus minimization or conversion to cyclosporine may be considered in collaboration with transplant teams. Metformin is generally avoided early post-transplant due to renal impairment risk. This cascade requires multidisciplinary management.
review_notes: Well-established cascade in transplant medicine. NFAT-mediated insulin gene expression impairment by tacrolimus is the accepted mechanism. Multifactorial etiology (tacrolimus + concurrent corticosteroids + genetic susceptibility) is correctly noted. Metformin contraindication early post-transplant (eGFR concerns, lactic acidosis risk) is accurate. Classified 'monitoring-related' and 'context_dependent': insulin treatment for PTDM is often appropriate; cascade occurs when tacrolimus minimization is not explored as a primary strategy. No field corrections required.

name: Cyclosporine-induced hyperuricemia and gout treated with colchicine or allopurinol
clinical_concern: Cyclosporine reduces renal urate excretion via OAT transporter inhibition. Critical DDI: colchicine toxicity (rhabdomyolysis, neuromyopathy, pancytopenia) is markedly increased by cyclosporine via combined P-glycoprotein and CYP3A4 inhibition — colchicine dose must be substantially reduced and duration limited. Allopurinol-azathioprine interaction (xanthine oxidase inhibition with TPMT pathway shift) is separately lethal in transplant patients. NSAIDs are contraindicated due to nephrotoxicity.
review_notes: Critical cascade due to potentially life-threatening DDIs. Colchicine toxicity with cyclosporine is well-documented: combined P-gp and CYP3A4 inhibition raises colchicine AUC by 2-3x, causing neuromyopathy, rhabdomyolysis, and pancytopenia — fatalities reported. Dose reduction to maximum 0.5 mg colchicine per course (with maximum 1.5 mg/course) and treatment duration limitation are essential safety requirements. Allopurinol-azathioprine interaction (xanthine oxidase inhibition → accumulated thiopurine metabolites → myelosuppression) is correctly flagged and is separately life-threatening. NSAIDs contraindicated due to combined nephrotoxicity. This cascade warrants a CRITICAL alert level. No field corrections required.

name: Loop diuretic-induced hypomagnesemia treated with magnesium supplements
clinical_concern: Loop diuretics inhibit NKCC2 transporter in the thick ascending limb of Henle, abolishing the electrochemical gradient required for passive paracellular magnesium reabsorption. Magnesium depletion potentiates hypokalemia and ventricular arrhythmias. Concurrent PPI use compounds magnesium loss synergistically. Loop diuretic dose minimization and dietary magnesium intake should be optimized alongside supplementation; refractory hypokalemia unresponsive to potassium supplementation should prompt magnesium level check.
review_notes: Well-established electrolyte cascade. NKCC2 inhibition mechanism for magnesium wasting in the thick ascending limb is pharmacologically correct. The clinical note on refractory hypokalemia not responding to potassium supplementation as a magnesium deficiency indicator is clinically important and accurate (Mg2+ deficiency impairs ROMK channel-mediated K+ reabsorption). PPI synergy for magnesium depletion is correctly and importantly noted. No field corrections required.

name: Loop diuretic-induced hypokalemia treated with potassium supplements or potassium-sparing diuretics
clinical_concern: Loop diuretics cause potassium wasting via increased distal nephron sodium delivery driving K+ secretion. In heart failure patients on concurrent ACE inhibitors or ARBs, added potassium supplementation creates significant hyperkalemia risk — a combination that requires careful electrolyte monitoring. Spironolactone in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction may address both hypokalemia and mortality benefit simultaneously, making it a rational substitution rather than addition.
review_notes: Well-established electrolyte cascade. Classified 'monitoring-related' and 'context_dependent': spironolactone in HFrEF is an evidence-based mortality-reducing therapy (RALES trial) and not an inappropriate cascade when used for its primary indication. Cascade occurs when drug-induced hypokalemia is not recognized and potassium supplementation is added without reviewing loop diuretic dose or indication. Hyperkalemia risk with RAAS inhibitors + potassium supplementation is correctly flagged. No field corrections required.

name: Fluoroquinolone-induced tendinopathy treated with NSAIDs
clinical_concern: Fluoroquinolones inhibit tenocyte proliferation and matrix metalloproteinase homeostasis, impairing tendon structural integrity via mechanisms independent of inflammation. NSAIDs treat symptom but do not address structural damage and may mask warning symptoms, increasing tendon rupture risk. Fluoroquinolone discontinuation and strict physical activity restriction are mandatory. Risk is amplified in elderly patients, those receiving concurrent corticosteroids, and solid organ transplant recipients.
review_notes: Cascade is pharmacologically valid. Fluoroquinolone-associated tendinopathy mechanism involves: chelation of metal ions (Mg2+, Zn2+) required for matrix metalloproteinase (MMP) function, disruption of collagen synthesis, inhibition of tenocyte proliferation, and induction of tenocyte apoptosis. These are independent of inflammation, making NSAIDs symptom-masking agents rather than mechanistic treatments — correctly stated. Risk amplification with concurrent corticosteroids and in transplant recipients is evidence-based (FDA Black Box Warning). Evidence_strength 'moderate' as cascade-specific epidemiological studies are limited. No field corrections required.

name: SSRI/SNRI-induced sexual dysfunction treated with PDE5 inhibitors
clinical_concern: Serotonin excess suppresses dopaminergic and noradrenergic pathways involved in sexual arousal. PDE5 inhibitors address erectile dysfunction only; libido reduction, anorgasmia, and delayed ejaculation are not improved. Interaction risk with nitrates is relevant in the cardiovascular patient population. Alternatives with lower sexual dysfunction burden include bupropion, mirtazapine, or dose reduction; a structured SSRI dose review should precede PDE5 inhibitor prescribing.
review_notes: Well-described cascade, particularly prevalent in male patients with depression and comorbid cardiovascular conditions. Serotonergic suppression of dopaminergic/noradrenergic sexual arousal pathways is the accepted mechanism. Critical limitation of PDE5 inhibitors correctly stated: they address only erectile dysfunction, not libido reduction, delayed ejaculation, or anorgasmia. Evidence_strength 'moderate' because sexual dysfunction is multifactorial (depression itself, relationship factors) and causal attribution to SSRI/SNRI requires careful temporal assessment. Bupropion (NDRI mechanism, lower serotonergic burden) as a switch strategy has evidence from RCTs. No field corrections required.

name: High-anticholinergic antipsychotic-induced orthostatic hypotension treated with vasopressors
clinical_concern: Antipsychotics with high alpha-1 adrenergic receptor affinity (clozapine, chlorpromazine, quetiapine, thioridazine) cause pronounced vasodilation and orthostatic hypotension. Risperidone has intermediate alpha-1 activity. NOTE: haloperidol has been removed from the trigger list — it has substantially lower alpha-1 affinity (Ki ~40 nM) than clozapine or chlorpromazine; orthostatic hypotension is uncommon with haloperidol at standard doses and its inclusion would generate high false-positive rates. Adding midodrine or fludrocortisone without reviewing antipsychotic agent and dose significantly increases cardiovascular burden and supine hypertension risk. Dose reduction and slow titration protocols should precede vasopressor addition.
review_notes: CORRECTION: Haloperidol is listed as a trigger with equivalent weight to clozapine and chlorpromazine, but this is pharmacologically inaccurate. Haloperidol has substantially lower alpha-1 adrenergic receptor affinity (Ki ~40 nM) compared to clozapine (Ki ~7 nM), chlorpromazine, quetiapine, or thioridazine. Haloperidol is a potent D2 antagonist with low alpha-1 activity; orthostatic hypotension is uncommon with haloperidol at standard doses. Its inclusion as an equal trigger inflates false-positive alerts significantly. Clinical_concern updated to stratify by alpha-1 affinity. Risperidone has intermediate alpha-1 activity. High-alpha-1 agents (clozapine, chlorpromazine, quetiapine, thioridazine) should trigger higher-priority alerts.

name: Clozapine-induced sialorrhea treated with systemic anticholinergics
clinical_concern: Clozapine-induced sialorrhea is paradoxical: despite broad anticholinergic effects, clozapine stimulates salivary secretion via partial M4 muscarinic agonism and alpha-2 adrenergic antagonism (reducing sympathetic inhibition of salivary glands). CRITICAL DISTINCTION in treatment_drug CNS penetration: hyoscine butylbromide and glycopyrronium are QUATERNARY AMMONIUM compounds (QACs) with minimal oral bioavailability and negligible CNS penetration — their contribution to central anticholinergic burden is minimal; these are acceptable for topical/local oral use. Oxybutynin (tertiary amine) readily crosses the BBB and substantially increases central anticholinergic burden, causing cognitive impairment and delirium — it is the most problematic agent in this list for schizophrenia patients. Preferred approach: topical sublingual anticholinergics (ipratropium, atropine ophthalmic drops) to minimize systemic and CNS exposure.
review_notes: Cascade is pharmacologically valid. Mechanism clarification: clozapine sialorrhea involves partial M4 muscarinic agonism (paradoxical salivation) combined with alpha-2 adrenergic antagonism (reducing sympathetic inhibition of salivary secretion) — this explanation is acceptable. CORRECTION: The treatment_drug list conflates agents with vastly different CNS penetration profiles. Hyoscine butylbromide and glycopyrronium are QUATERNARY AMMONIUM compounds (QACs): highly ionized at physiological pH, negligible oral bioavailability (3-5%), and minimal CNS penetration — their contribution to systemic anticholinergic CNS burden is much lower than implied by the clinical_concern. Oxybutynin (tertiary amine) readily crosses the CNS and IS the problematic agent in this context. Clinical_concern updated to differentiate. Topical/sublingual delivery (ipratropium, atropine 1% ophthalmic drops applied sublingually) is the preferred approach — correctly noted.

name: Clozapine-induced constipation treated with laxatives
clinical_concern: Clozapine has the highest anticholinergic burden of any antipsychotic and causes severe, potentially life-threatening constipation including paralytic ileus and intestinal obstruction (reported mortality). Reactive treatment of established constipation with laxatives is a cascade that occurs because bowel monitoring protocols are often absent. Active, protocol-driven prophylactic laxative prescription and regular bowel function assessment should be standard practice during clozapine treatment. Late recognition may require surgical intervention.
review_notes: Critical cascade due to potential for fatal outcome (paralytic ileus, bowel perforation). Clozapine has the highest anticholinergic burden of all antipsychotics. Classified 'context_dependent' for appropriateness: prophylactic laxative co-prescription is RECOMMENDED standard of care in most clozapine guidelines and is therefore NOT inherently inappropriate. The cascade occurs when bowel monitoring protocols are absent and constipation presents late (reactive rather than proactive management). The most dangerous aspect is failure to recognize early signs of bowel obstruction, not laxative use per se. A CDS alert should trigger proactive bowel monitoring protocol initiation at clozapine start. No field corrections required.

name: Activating antidepressant-induced insomnia treated with sedative-hypnotics
clinical_concern: Activating antidepressants (fluoxetine, venlafaxine, bupropion, desvenlafaxine) frequently cause insomnia via noradrenergic and serotonergic activation, particularly when dosed in the evening. Duloxetine has a mixed profile: noradrenergic activation may cause insomnia in some patients but sedation in others — retained as trigger with this caveat. CORRECTIONS: 'trazodone low-dose' and 'mirtazapine low-dose' removed from treatment_drug: both are antidepressants with distinct mechanisms; prescribing them as 'sleep aids' while the patient is on an SSRI/SNRI creates two-antidepressant polypharmacy, adds serotonergic burden, and introduces separate pharmacological complexity not appropriate for cascade detection classification. Z-drugs (zolpidem, zopiclone) substantially increase fall risk and cognitive impairment in elderly patients. Low-dose quetiapine for sleep adds metabolic, cardiovascular, and EPS risk. Primary intervention: morning dosing of the activating antidepressant.
review_notes: Cascade is pharmacologically valid. CORRECTIONS APPLIED: (1) 'trazodone low-dose' and 'mirtazapine low-dose' removed from treatment_drug. Both are antidepressants, not sedative-hypnotics; prescribing either for insomnia while a patient is already on an SSRI/SNRI creates polypharmacy (two antidepressants), adds serotonergic burden, and introduces separate pharmacological complexity. They must not be categorized as sedative-hypnotics in a cascade detection system. (2) 'duloxetine' note: duloxetine has a mixed profile — noradrenergic activation can cause insomnia, but it also causes sedation in a significant proportion of patients. Evening dosing is a common clinical error. Retained in trigger_drug with clinical caveat. (3) 'desvenlafaxine' retained: SNRI with predominantly noradrenergic profile, clearly activating. Z-drug risks in elderly correctly noted.

name: Systemic corticosteroid-induced psychiatric symptoms treated with antipsychotics or mood stabilizers
clinical_concern: Corticosteroid psychiatric effects are dose-dependent (significantly more common at >40 mg prednisone equivalent/day) and typically dose-reversible. Adding antipsychotics or mood stabilizers without tapering the corticosteroid adds metabolic and extrapyramidal burden and may make it harder to attribute symptom resolution to drug change vs. steroid reduction. Steroid dose reduction, when clinically feasible, should be the primary intervention.
review_notes: Cascade is pharmacologically valid and clinically underrecognized. Dose-dependent neuropsychiatric effects (more common at >40 mg prednisolone equivalent/day) via glucocorticoid receptor-mediated effects on hippocampal and limbic circuits is accepted mechanism. Classified 'context_dependent': antipsychotic treatment may be necessary for severe steroid psychosis or mania to ensure patient safety when dose reduction is not immediately feasible; cascade occurs when steroid tapering as primary strategy is not pursued. Evidence_strength 'moderate': case series and cohort data; no RCTs specifically addressing this cascade. No field corrections required.

name: Organic nitrate-induced headache treated with analgesics
clinical_concern: Nitrate headache results from cGMP-mediated cerebral vasodilation; it typically attenuates with continued use as nitrate tolerance develops. NSAIDs in patients with established ischemic heart disease increase cardiovascular risk. Chronic daily analgesic use (>10-15 days/month) causes medication-overuse headache. Nitrate formulation review (asymmetric dosing to preserve nitrate-free interval), dose adjustment, or review of nitrate indication should be performed before analgesic introduction.
review_notes: Cascade is pharmacologically valid. cGMP-mediated cerebral vasodilation mechanism is correctly described. Classified 'context_dependent': nitrate headache typically attenuates with tolerance (within days to weeks of regular dosing) and may not always require pharmacological treatment. Chronic analgesic use (>10-15 days/month) causing medication-overuse headache is an important second-order cascade risk correctly noted. NSAIDs in ischemic heart disease patients add cardiovascular risk. Evidence_strength 'moderate': well-described pharmacological mechanism but limited cascade-specific epidemiological data. No field corrections required.

name: Metformin-induced vitamin B12 deficiency treated with cyanocobalamin supplementation
clinical_concern: Metformin competitively inhibits calcium-dependent intrinsic factor-B12 complex absorption in the terminal ileum. B12 deficiency may manifest as peripheral neuropathy indistinguishable from diabetic neuropathy, leading to escalation of neuropathic pain treatment rather than B12 correction. Annual B12 monitoring is recommended in long-term metformin users (>4 years). Parenteral B12 is preferred when significant neuropathy is present, as oral absorption remains impaired while metformin continues.
review_notes: Well-established cascade, particularly important in aging populations with long-term T2DM. Mechanism note: the description 'competitively inhibits calcium-dependent intrinsic factor-B12 complex absorption' is a simplified but acceptable description. More precisely, metformin interferes with calcium-dependent membrane action at the ileal brush border (cubilin receptor function), impairing the binding and endocytosis of the IF-B12 complex — not direct competitive inhibition. The clinical_concern's emphasis on the neuropathy overlap (B12 deficiency vs. diabetic neuropathy) is clinically critical and often leads to escalation of gabapentinoids or analgesics instead of B12 correction — a nested cascade. Annual B12 monitoring recommendation aligns with NICE and ADA guidance. No field corrections required.

name: Non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker-induced constipation treated with laxatives
clinical_concern: Verapamil inhibits intestinal smooth muscle L-type voltage-gated calcium channels, directly reducing intestinal peristalsis; constipation affects up to 25-30% of verapamil users. Adding laxatives without reviewing verapamil necessity, dose, or switch to a dihydropyridine CCB (which lacks significant GI smooth muscle effect) adds drug burden unnecessarily. In elderly patients already on anticholinergic agents, combined GI motility impairment increases fecal impaction and obstruction risk.
review_notes: Well-established cascade. Verapamil inhibits L-type voltage-gated calcium channels in intestinal smooth muscle, reducing peristaltic activity. Incidence of constipation with verapamil (25-40%) is among the highest of any cardiovascular drug. Diltiazem carries lower constipation risk than verapamil. Adding laxatives without reviewing verapamil dose or substituting with a DHP-CCB (equivalent antihypertensive/antianginal efficacy, no GI smooth muscle effect) increases drug burden unnecessarily. Compounding risk with concurrent anticholinergic agents is accurately noted. No field corrections required.
